published_date: 2026-05-27
video_id: koWrV9ykBEo
url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=koWrV9ykBEo
title: The Macro Minute: Will ending the Strait of Hormuz Crisis be a sell-the-news catalyst?

Happy Wednesday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dellaverson,
on our macro minute for Wednesday, May
27th, 2026. As always, we'll start with
the executive summary from today's
lead-off morning note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
will ending the Strait of Hormuz crisis
be a sell-the-news catalyst? Our answer
is probably not, given that the SEC is
essentially seeking to fast-track
several massive tech IPOs, which would
pull forward the impact of the sharp
increases in public equity supply on the
stock market. For what it's worth, we
believe the federal government will do
whatever it takes, to borrow a phrase
from Mario Draghi, to support markets
into this year's AI IPOs, as global AI
dominance is core to the Trump
administration's economic agenda and
geopolitical strategy.
This comes amid Iranian state television
disclosing details of an unofficial
draft memorandum between Tehran and
Washington, which states the US will end
its naval blockade of Iran and withdraw
naval forces from waters surrounding
Iran.
That stock and bond markets are having a
muted response to these developments
amid sell-offs in precious metals and
crypto is concerning and speaks to the
risk that we highlighted in recent weeks
regarding the reopening of the Strait of
Hormuz being a sell-the-news catalyst.
Conversely, earnings trends and
seasonality signal upside risk. Notably,
seasonality since 1990 indicates the VIX
has historically declined to intra-year
lows in June and July before rebounding
in August and September. As always,
we'll wrap up with a question from our
community here in the 42 macro
dashboard. Uh this one's titled
Truflation. Uh it says, "Skipper, what
do you make of Truflation at only 2.19%
even post-oil uh ramp?" Uh so, the first
thing I'll say on this is we don't make
anything of Truflation. Uh there's a
variety of reasons why we don't care
about Truflation, most of which I've
already covered in this program and in
other uh programs. Uh number one, uh
neither the markets nor the Fed care
about Truflation. Truflation does not
move uh the global fixed income market,
which obviously is the the core pillar
of global financial markets. And so, we
don't focus on that because the fixed
income global fixed income markets don't
focus on that. Number two, the Fed,
which lags movements in global fixed
income markets, don't focus on
Truflation. And so, as a function of
neither the global fixed income markets
nor the Fed caring about Truflation,
we're not going to waste our time caring
about Truflation. It's not to say
Truflation is not a
a good measurement or even thoughtful
measurement. It obviously is a you know,
well-constructed index. It differs
markedly from BLS CPI and the
BEA's PCE deflator statistics. So, be
aware that there's a you know, for
instance, there's a lot more goods
there's a bigger weighting in goods to
Truflation. Truflation doesn't make
hedonic adjustments like CPI and PCE
deflator do. So, there's a lot of you
know, kind of
you know, I would say wonky methodo-
methodological reasons for why
Truflation can diverge materially from
CPI or or PCE deflator. And those
divergences, in my opinion, in our
opinion, and in the market's opinion,
make Truflation a just a you know, quite
frankly, not a very good statistic to be
you know, you know, allocating your
focus budget to. It's much more
important to allocate your focus budget
to the inflation statistics that the
market, the fixed income market cares
about, as well as the Federal Reserve.
So, we'll wrap it up there. Terry
Sandher presenting our macro minute for
Wednesday, May 27th, 2026. Best of luck
out there today. We'll catch you back
here tomorrow. Cheers.
